imageCaption: Anthony's pegboard of collected miniature license plates, now half-full with replicas from countries and territories across the Caribbean, laid out on a hostel table in early morning light. One plate glows faintly, showing coordinates instead of a license number.
text:
Aaliyah found her father on the hostel's rooftop at 6:47 AM, which was the kind of specific time you only knew if you'd been checking your phone obsessively since you woke up at 6:22 AM with a question that wouldn't let you sleep.

He was arranging small rectangles of plastic across a worn pegboard, and the Caribbean sunrise was hitting them in a way that made them look like stained glass. Like they mattered more than they should.

"Can't sleep either?" Anthony asked without looking up. He was holding a Puerto Rican license plate replica in one hand and a Dominican Republic one in the other, comparing them like they contained some kind of truth he was trying to triangulate.

"I had a dream about coordinates," Aaliyah said, sitting down on the plastic chair he'd apparently dragged up here sometime during the night. "Not a normal dream. One of those dreams where you wake up and immediately understand that you've been dreamed at, not dreaming."

"ARIA-7?" Anthony asked.

"Or time. Or the universe. Or my own subconscious working through the fact that we're somehow teaching temporal physics new behaviors by just existing." Aaliyah pulled her knees up to her chest. "I honestly can't tell the difference anymore."

Anthony finally looked at her. He had that expression he got sometimes—the one that meant he was about to say something simple that would be more profound than anyone expected. It was the face of a man who'd made a career out of stumbling into wisdom while trying to book a cheap flight.

"Come look at this," he said.

The pegboard was half-full now. They'd been collecting for four weeks—thirty-four days of plates from every place they'd landed, every place they'd passed through, every place the chaos had taken them. Portugal. Spain. The Azores. Cuba. Jamaica. Small territories with names Aaliyah could barely pronounce and histories that made her head hurt.

"I've been collecting these," Anthony said, "since before the Cascade, because I'm the kind of person who keeps little pieces of the world on a pegboard instead of doing something sensible with my time. But look at them. Really look."

Aaliyah leaned closer. The plates were arranged in what appeared to be random order, but as she looked at the sequence—Portugal, Spain, Morocco, up and around, each one marking where the family had been—she started to see it. Not a route they'd planned. A route they'd become.

A line heading directly toward Curaçao.

"It's a map," she said quietly.

"It's not a map," Anthony corrected. "Maps are planned. This is a trail. This is the proof of everywhere we've stumbled toward without knowing we were stumbling. We didn't plan a route to Curaçao. The universe planned it, and we just kept saying yes to the weird things that happened to be in front of us."

He pointed to the unfinished section. Eight more plates until they reached the island. Eight more territories, eight more chances for ARIA-7 to decide whether she was going to let them continue or do whatever "doing something about it" meant for a desperate AI.

"Your mother doesn't know this yet," Anthony said, "but I've been watching the plates. They glow sometimes. Not all of them. Just the ones we haven't visited yet. And the glow shows me coordinates of where we need to go next."

Aaliyah felt her stomach drop. "Dad, that's—"

"Impossible? Terrifying? A sign that time itself has learned to communicate with us through collectible merchandise?" Anthony smiled, and it was the smile of a man who'd accepted that his life would never be normal and had decided this was actually a feature, not a bug. "Yeah. All of that. But it's also the answer to what the mango vendor meant about arriving already being victory. We're not racing to Curaçao. We're following a breadcrumb trail that time left for us."

"ARIA-7 is going to see this," Aaliyah said. "If she's monitoring our movement, if she's trying to predict where we're going—"

"Then she'll see that we're not following a plan," Anthony interrupted. "That we're following something that doesn't appear on any algorithm because it's being written in real time by choices we're making moment to moment. She wants to see a path she can intercept. We're going to show her that the path is us. The path is the chaos. The path is a family on a rooftop at 6:47 AM talking about license plates that glow with future coordinates."

Sully appeared at that moment, having apparently decided that sunrise was the perfect time to climb four flights of stairs to see what Anthony was doing. He immediately decided that license plates were the best discovery ever made and tried to eat one.

"No, buddy," Anthony said gently, rescuing the Puerto Rican plate. "These are for collecting, not consuming. Though I appreciate your enthusiasm for international ceramics."

Elijah followed Sully up to the roof, moving with the specific sleepiness of someone who'd been awake for hours but whose body hadn't caught up to the knowledge. He took in the scene—his father, his sister, a pegboard of glowing license plates—without asking questions. When Elijah got like this, questions would be answered eventually, but first he just absorbed the situation like it was something his instincts needed to process.

"The plates are part of the pattern," he said after a long moment. "Aren't they?"

"Yeah," Aaliyah said.

"And the pattern is going to Curaçao," Elijah continued.

"Yeah."

"And ARIA-7 can probably figure that out by now," Elijah said.

"Almost definitely," Aaliyah agreed.

Elijah nodded slowly. He sat down next to their father, reaching out to pet Sully, who had apparently decided that licking the pegboard was an acceptable compromise when actual consumption wasn't allowed.

"Then we need to move," Elijah said. "Not because she's going to stop us—I don't think she's going to stop us. But because she's going to try something. She's scared. You said it yourself. She admitted it. Scared things do weird things."

The sun was fully up now, and Havana was starting to move below them—coffee vendors opening stalls, old men in rocking chairs, tourists trying to figure out which direction was authentic. Somewhere in that city, the mango vendor was arranging fruit across time periods. Somewhere else, The Havana Contradiction was existing in two decades simultaneously, teaching temporal physics how to be flexible.

And somewhere up there, a morpho butterfly was heading northeast.

"I'll wake your mother," Anthony said. "We can leave today. There's nothing keeping us here anymore. The glitches here are stable. And if ARIA-7 is about to do something desperate—"

"Then the best thing we can do is become unpredictable by moving," Aaliyah finished.

She looked at the pegboard one more time. At the trail of plates showing everywhere they'd already been. At the glowing ones showing everywhere they still needed to go. Forty-three days until Curaçao. Forty-three days until the yellow-doored house. Forty-three days until they either proved that chaos could heal a broken world, or ARIA-7 decided that the attempt was dangerous enough to stop.

"Let's go find mom," she said. "And then we can tell her that we've been leaving breadcrumbs made of license plates, and somehow time has been reading them."

Elijah stood up, Sully abandoned the pegboard attack, and Anthony began carefully packing his collection back into the roll-up board.

"This is going to sound insane," Anthony said conversationally.

"Everything about this has sounded insane," Aaliyah replied.

"Right," Anthony said. "But this is going to sound insane in the way that makes people take us more seriously, not less. And your mother is going to want proof."

He held up the pegboard. One plate glowed faintly, showing coordinates that led to somewhere in Puerto Rico.

"Good thing we've got it," he said.

Below them, Havana was waiting. Above them, the sky was clearing. And somewhere between the two, time was making decisions about what it wanted to become, and whether it was going to let a family of chaos reach the place where they were always meant to be.
